channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: 8 Are Rescued From Texas Roller Coaster Stuck Almost 100 Feet Off the Ground
item.description: The riders waited for about four hours, facing up, on the roller coaster at the Pleasure Pier park in Galveston. There were no injuries, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado

item.title: Scripps Spelling Bee 2026 Takeaways: Shrey Parikh Wins in Spell-Off
item.description: The competition came down to a spell-off. Contestants were eliminated early, and a sixth grader became a crowd favorite.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shrey Parikh, a 14-year-old from Rancho Cucamonga, Calif., broke the record for the most words spelled correctly in a spell-off.

item.title: Families Settle Suit Over Deaths in Baltimore Bridge Collapse
item.description: The owner and the operator of the cargo ship that crashed into the Key Bridge settled cases brought by several victims’ families, but other claims are headed to trial.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Pete Kiehart for The New York Times
item.media:description: The families of five of the six men killed in the Key Bridge collapse two years ago announced a settlement with the cargo ship’s owner and operator.

item.title: 3 Dead After Large Fire at Dallas Apartment Building
item.description: A damaged gas line led to a five-alarm fire in the Oak Cliff neighborhood. At least four were injured, and firefighters believe they may find more victims.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez

item.title: In Carroll Lawsuits Inquiry, Scrutiny Turns Toward Private Citizens Who Antagonized Trump
item.description: The Justice Department is said to be examining the funding of lawsuits brought by E. Jean Carroll, an author who has never sought a public role, political power or governmental authority.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll, the author and columnist, leaving court in Manhattan in 2024 after a hearing on one of her lawsuits against Donald J. Trump.

item.title: J.F.K. Airport to Begin Screening Some Passengers for Ebola
item.description: The U.S. has begun rerouting travelers from the Democratic Republic of Congo, South Sudan and Uganda to select airports for enhanced health screenings, to identify those who may have been exposed to the Ebola virus.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie/The New York Times
item.media:description: Travelers pass through John F. Kennedy International Airport, which will begin screening passengers from Ebola-stricken areas.

item.title: After Traffic Stop for Phone in Right Hand, She Called Out the Problem
item.description: The deputy insisted he saw a driver with a phone in her right hand. That turned out to be impossible, and the case was dropped.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

item.title: Death Toll Rises in Washington Paper Mill Explosion
item.description: At least eight are now confirmed dead, and firefighters believe there are three more bodies they have yet to reach, making the crisis the worst industrial disaster in the state’s modern history.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin and Aaron West
item.media:credit: Mathieu Lewis-Rolland/Getty Images
item.media:description: Community members placed electric candles at a vigil at R.A. Long Park after a fatal chemical storage tank failure in Longview, Wash.

item.title: Suit Says Black Infants Were Subjected to Experimental Vaccine Without Consent
item.description: The babies were part of a vaccine trial for a respiratory virus in the 1960s and died shortly after. Their families said they had been unaware of the trial until recently.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Benjamin Crump is representing the families of two Black infants who were unknowingly enrolled as test subjects in a mid-1960s vaccine trial for R.S.V.

item.title: Trump’s Iran Approach Sows Confusion
item.description: President Trump’s pendulum swings on Iran have often seemed driven by mood and moment rather than any discernible strategy.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s shifts reflect a political tug of war between hawkish supporters urging him to hit Iran harder and noninterventionists urging him to make a quick deal.

item.title: Alaska Authorities Race to Rescue 4 Climbers Who Fell on Mount McKinley
item.description: The condition of the climbers was not immediately known.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Becky Bohrer/Associated Press
item.media:description: Weather conditions on the mountain were improving enough to allow for helicopter operations, officials said.

item.title: Who Are the 9 Scripps Spelling Bee Finalists?
item.description: The remaining spellers are 12 to 14, and represent five states as well as the District of Columbia.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert/Associated Press

item.title: The spelling bee takes a game show approach to combat declining viewership.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak
item.media:credit: Jose Luis Magana/Associated Press
item.media:description: Subtle changes in the positioning of the spellers onstage, as well as camera tricks, are intended to bring “game show sensibilities” to viewers.

item.title: From ‘clickbait’ to ‘nerfing,’ modern slang is shaking up the bee.
item.description: Internet slang and non-European terms are appearing more and more in the competition while words with Greek and Latin words are being used less.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer
item.media:credit: Jose Luis Magana/Associated Press
item.media:description: The list of 4,000 dictionary words that the Scripps National Spelling Bee uses is updated every year, with about 20 percent of them rotating out.

item.title: Mina Kimes, an N.F.L. analyst, is returning to her spelling bee roots.
item.description: A former school spelling bee champ, Ms. Kimes is on a mission to show America that these spellers are just as awesome as Patrick Mahomes.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.media:credit: Chris Pizzello/Associated Press
item.media:description: A former school spelling bee champ, Ms. Kimes is on a mission to show America that the contestants are just as awesome as Patrick Mahomes.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak

item.title: A Democrat’s Dodge on AIPAC Points to the Party’s Tensions Over Israel
item.description: A key moment at a Senate primary debate in Michigan showed how pro-Israel Democrats are approaching the issue with caution.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Danielle James/The Flint Journal, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Mallory McMorrow, Haley Stevens and Dr. Abdul El-Sayed are locked in a tight Democratic primary race for Senate in Michigan.

item.title: After an Explosion at a Washington Paper Mill, Mourning Mixes With Dread
item.description: The disaster at the mill in Longview, Wash., which took at least eight lives, was greeted with sadness but not surprise by a community that knew cutbacks and other pressures had raised risks.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin and Aaron West
item.media:credit: Mathieu Lewis-Rolland/Getty Images
item.media:description: A vigil on Tuesday for the victims of a fatal chemical storage tank failure in Longview, Wash.

item.title: Iran, Gas Prices, the Ballroom: Trump Professes Indifference Over Fallout
item.description: President Trump has increasingly adopted a posture of nonchalance when pushed on the political consequences of his actions.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: “I don’t care about the midterms,” President Trump said at a cabinet meeting on Wednesday.

item.title: Swallowing Concerns, G.O.P. Senators Rally Behind Paxton
item.description: After attacking the Texas attorney general, who won the G.O.P. nomination, Senate Republicans are pivoting sharply to support him in what is now a competitive race.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general, beat Senator John Cornyn by close to 30 points on Tuesday night, transforming Texas into a battleground state.

item.title: Where Do E. Jean Carroll’s Lawsuits Against President Trump Stand?
item.description: Two monetary judgments against Mr. Trump are winding their way through the legal system, with the Supreme Court likely to be the final word on both.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll leaves court after closing arguments in her defamation trial against Donald Trump in 2023.

item.title: Quarantine Unit for Americans Exposed to Ebola to Open in Kenya Friday
item.description: But U.S. officials are still working to find centers in Europe to accept Americans who need advanced treatment, seen as critical to improving odds of survival.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian and Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo is already estimated to have caused more than 1,000 cases and more than 200 deaths since it was announced May 15.

item.title: Memphis Residents Sue Federal Agents Over Arrest Tactics Amid Crime and Immigration Crackdown
item.description: A federal task force in the city has been targeting crime and undocumented immigrants since October. Observers say they have faced retaliation for trying to track the agents’ work.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal agents conducted a traffic stop in January in Memphis.

item.title: Supreme Court Says Death Row Inmate Can Challenge Exclusion of Black Jurors
item.description: Terry Pitchford was convicted in 2006 for his role in the murder of a shopkeeper by a 12-member jury that included one Black member.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: via Joseph Perkovich
item.media:description: The Supreme Court decision means that Mr. Pitchford, 40, who has served death row for more than 20 years, is entitled to a new trial.

item.title: Watchdog Agency Seeks to Reverse Its Win Against Firm Run by Winklevoss Twins
item.description: The Commodity Futures Trading Commission filed jointly with a company run by the Winklevoss twins to overturn their settlement.
item.dc:creator: Sharon LaFraniere
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Gemini’s founders, Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss, are among the president’s closest allies in the crypto industry.

item.title: Dell Gets a $9.7 Billion Defense Contract. Trump’s Portfolio Stands to Benefit.
item.description: The president’s family says his stock purchases are automatically timed, but recent acquisitions of the computer company’s shares raise questions.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael and Susan Dell pledged in December to donate $6.25 billion to kick off the “Trump accounts” program that creates special investment savings accounts with tax benefits for American children.

item.title: Is Trump the President the Framers Feared?
item.description: The drafters of the Constitution hoped for energetic but accountable presidents.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: A painting of the signing of the Constitution hangs on the House side of the Capitol.

item.title: Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer Will Not Run for President in 2028
item.description: The Michigan governor made the remarks to a reporter on Thursday morning. Later, she sounded less definitive. Ms. Whitmer has been viewed as part of a field of possible Democratic candidates.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Gretchen Whitmer of Michigan at an event in Detroit in April.

item.title: Judge Declines, for Now, to Block Mail-In Voting Changes Ordered by Trump
item.description: The ruling allowed the Trump administration to continue to pursue changes that include enlisting the Postal Service in checking voters against a national database.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: A voter casting a mail-in ballot in June.

item.title: These Are Some of the Trump Justice Dept.’s Targets
item.description: When he returned to office, President Trump vowed revenge against those he said had challenged or defied him. Here’s an incomplete list of the people his Justice Department has targeted.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Department of Justice in Washington, this year.

item.title: Texas School Police Investigation: 5 Takeaways
item.description: The state’s ambitious school policing initiative has few safeguards to prevent overreach by officers.
item.dc:creator: Clare Amari, Kristian Hernández and Asher Lehrer-Small
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anabelle Jaramillo is among the thousands of students in Texas who have had physical encounters with school police officers in recent years.

item.title: These Drivers Found Cheap Gas
item.description: It’s in Valley Center, Calif., on tribal land. Customers relish every penny saved.
item.dc:creator: Corina Knoll
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Dickerson was among those who stopped at Horizon Fuel Center recently when its gas was going for $5.39 a gallon, well below the state average.

item.title: ‘Vibrant as Ever’: Harvard’s Jewish Life Thrives as Trump Investigates
item.description: Trump officials have focused on episodes during protests over the war in Gaza at Harvard and other schools. Jews on campus describe a changed atmosphere.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: At Harvard University, students and others say the culture is different from the one depicted in the Trump administration’s lawsuit.

item.title: Our Reporter on What Happened When Texas Increased School Police
item.description: After the 2022 mass shooting in Uvalde, Texas, the state passed a law mandating police presence in schools. Our reporter Clare Amari describes how some students in Texas have experienced physical force by school police officers since then.
item.dc:creator: Clare Amari, Christina Shaman, Gilad Thaler, Lauren Pruitt and Joey Sendaydiego

item.title: ‘Both Parties Kind of Get It Wrong’: The Young Men Who May Swing the Midterms
item.description: Many Gen Z men who voted for Donald Trump are dismayed by his time in office. But they say they are not hearing an appealing pitch from Democrats, either.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning and Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times
